mūla.devanāgarī: निहत्य धार्तराष्ट्रान् नः का प्रीतिः स्याज् जनार्दन | पापम् एवाश्रयेद् अस्मान् हत्वैतान् आततायिनः
mūla.iast: nihatya dhārtarāṣṭrān naḥ kā prītiḥ syāj janārdana | pāpam evāśrayed asmān hatvaitān ātatāyinaḥ
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 1 (Arjuna-Viṣāda-Yoga (The Yoga of Arjuna's Despondency)), verse 36
mūla.speaker: Arjuna

advaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita.english_rendering: Śaṅkarācārya does not comment on BG 1.36; his bhāṣya opens at 2.10. The rendering here is extrapolated from his frame, not anchored in primary bhāṣya prose — flagged accordingly. Arjuna names two consequences of slaying the Dhārtarāṣṭras: the absence of any joy (prīti) that could justify the act, and the accrual of sin (pāpa) that would cling to the slayers. From a jñāna-mārga vantage, these two objections are pre-philosophical — rooted in ahaṃkāra (the 'I-maker') confusing the body-aggregate with the Self. The verse is thus, for Advaita, a specimen of ajñāna (non-cognition of the real), not a dharma-argument to be adjudicated.
advaita.divergence_note: ABSENT — Śaṅkara's commentary begins at 2.10; rendering extrapolated from Advaita frame only.

viśiṣṭādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
viśiṣṭādvaita.english_rendering: Rāmānuja's commentary here describes Arjuna's total collapse — sweat-drenched limbs, bow and arrows slipping from his grasp, sinking onto the chariot-floor — rather than parsing the verse word by word. The verse captures the voice of Arjuna as paramakāruṇika (supremely compassionate), a soul whose love for his kin (bandhu-sneha) and terror of dharmic transgression are both real responses of a cit bound in right relationship. In Viśiṣṭādvaita, even this grief is not mere error: it is the soul's authentic recoil from auspicious action performed for ignoble ends, and Bhagavān will redirect it, not erase it.
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: Rāmānuja on 1.36 describes Arjuna as paramakāruṇika, dīrghabandhu, paramādhārmika — his pity and grief at slaying those marked for death are foregrounded rather than rebutted.

dvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
dvaita.english_rendering: Madhvācārya does not comment on BG 1.36; his bhāṣya opens at 2.11. The rendering here is extrapolated from his frame, not anchored in primary bhāṣya prose — flagged accordingly. For Dvaita, the verse records a jīva in the grip of tamas and rājas — falsely attributing autonomous moral agency to itself ('sin would cling to us') rather than recognizing that all action and its fruit belong to Hari alone. Arjuna's appeal to ātatayin-dharma is legitimate on its face, but his conclusion that no joy could follow is a category error: bliss (ānanda) accrues from Hari-kainkarya, not from outcomes a finite jīva can calculate.
dvaita.divergence_note: ABSENT — Madhva's commentary begins at 2.11; rendering extrapolated from Dvaita frame only.

śuddhādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
śuddhādvaita.english_rendering: Vallabhācārya does not comment on BG 1.34–1.37 (the source notes this range as absent). The rendering here is extrapolated from his frame, not anchored in primary bhāṣya prose — flagged accordingly. In Puṣṭi-mārga, every scene of the Gītā is Kṛṣṇa's own līlā — even Arjuna's anguish is not the jīva's authentic sorrow but the play of Bhagavān drawing his devotee into deeper surrender. Arjuna's cry — 'what joy (prīti) could come to us, O Janārdana?' — is unwittingly the right question: prīti, in its fullest form, is Kṛṣṇa-prīti, the bliss of prasāda, which no battlefield outcome can supply or withhold.
śuddhādvaita.divergence_note: ABSENT — Vallabha's commentary does not cover 1.34–1.37; rendering extrapolated from Puṣṭi-mārga frame only.

bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
bhakti.english_rendering: Śrīdhara Svāmī anchors the verse in a jurisprudential debate: the smṛti rule that slaying an ātatayin (six-fold aggressor: arsonist, poisoner, armed attacker, plunderer, land-seizer, wife-abductor) is blameless is cited and then immediately qualified by Yājñavalkya's hierarchy — dharmaśāstra overrides artha-śāstra, and where two smṛtis conflict, the reasoned (nyāya) reading of the stronger śāstra prevails. Because these particular ātatāyins are Āryas (men of standing) and kin, Śrīdhara holds their slaying falls outside the protection of the ātatayin rule; pāpa would accrue. The verse is thus a careful legal argument, not pure emotion — Arjuna is applying dharmaśāstra correctly on one level, even as Kṛṣṇa will later reveal the deeper frame.
bhakti.divergence_note: Śrīdhara cites the six-fold ātatayin list and the ātatayin-vadha permission, then counterposes Yājñavalkya on smṛti-hierarchy: artha-śāstra is weaker than dharma-śāstra; dharma-śāstra-from-nyāya is strongest. The pāpa-claim follows from this hierarchy, not from sentiment.

advaita-bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita-bhakti.english_rendering: Madhusūdana Sarasvatī closes the block begun at 'na ca śreyo 'nupaśyāmi' by identifying two distinct harms in slaying kin: the absence of adṛṣṭa-phala (any unseen/karmic benefit) and the positive arising of anarttha (harm, ruin). The epithet 'Mādhava' — bearer of Lakṣmī — is not decorative; Madhusūdana reads it as an implicit argument: as Lakṣmī-pati, Kṛṣṇa cannot sanction an action that is alakṣmīka (inauspicious, Lakṣmī-repelling). Jñāna and bhakti fuse here: the metaphysical futility of the act and the devotional impropriety of asking the Auspicious One to bless it are a single indictment.
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Madhusūdana: adṛṣṭa-phala-abhāva (no unseen fruit) + anarttha-sambhava (harm will arise); Mādhava = Lakṣmī-pati, therefore this inauspicious (alakṣmīka) action should not be urged by him.

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: When a rule (e.g., 'you may kill an aggressor') explicitly permits an action, what makes Arjuna — and Śrīdhara after him — invoke a higher śāstra to override it? What is the structure of that override?
Arjuna appeals to Janārdana ('stirrer of men,' the one who agitates for right action') — does the choice of epithet commit him to the very engagement he is refusing?
Śrīdhara's Yājñavalkya citation places dharma-śāstra above artha-śāstra — how does this hierarchy map onto modern conflicts between law (artha-śāstra) and ethics (dharma-śāstra)?
Madhusūdana reads 'Mādhava' as a theological argument embedded in a name — how often do appellations in Sanskrit philosophical dialogue carry argumentative weight that translation erases?

Three schools (Advaita, Dvaita, Śuddhādvaita) have no bhāṣya here: what does the commentarial silence at Chapter 1 tell us about each school's view of where the Gītā's philosophical work actually begins?
If prīti (joy, delight) is the criterion Arjuna uses to evaluate the action, is he already in a proto-bhakti frame — measuring worth by relational delight rather than duty alone?
The verse pairs 'no joy will come' with 'sin will cling' — are these the same claim stated twice, or two genuinely distinct objections? How do the schools that do comment treat their relationship?

everyday_applications.advaita: Before any high-stakes confrontation, pause to ask: who is the 'we' that fears the sin, and who is the 'I' that wants the joy? Locating those as constructs of ahaṃkāra — rather than fixed entities to be protected — can dissolve the paralysis without requiring you to suppress the concern.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: When your compassion for those who will be hurt by a necessary decision causes grief, do not dismiss the grief as weakness: it is the soul's authentic tenderness. Bring it intact to Bhagavān, as Arjuna brings his sweat-drenched anguish to Kṛṣṇa — let the relationship redirect, not erase, the feeling.
everyday_applications.dvaita: In any ethical dilemma, notice the moment you say 'sin would cling to me' — and ask whether you are treating moral accounting as if you were the ultimate ledger-keeper. Hari holds the account; your work is to act as his dependent, not to audit consequences you cannot fully see.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: When you find yourself asking 'what joy could come from this?' in a moment of conflict, listen carefully — that question, however despairing it sounds, is pointing you toward the only joy that is real: the relational delight (prīti) of living in Kṛṣṇa's presence. Use the question as a doorway, not a dead end.
everyday_applications.bhakti: In professional or civic disputes, Śrīdhara's jurisprudential move applies: identify which level of rules you are operating under (policy, law, ethics, dharma), check the hierarchy explicitly, and do not let the lower-level permission ('you are allowed to do this') override the higher-level prohibition. Document the hierarchy, as he does, rather than leaving it implicit.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: When seeking support from a mentor or leader for a decision that troubles you, notice which epithet — which aspect of their role — you invoke. Madhusūdana's reading of 'Mādhava' suggests that naming the auspicious dimension of someone's identity implicitly calls them to uphold it; you can use this consciously in requesting guidance.
